coredata.dc:title: A simple and sensitive assay of alkaline phosphatase activity in serum by fluorescent silicon nanoparticles based on inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Dan
Jiang, Yanxiao
Chen, Sihan
Zhao, Qingnan
Zhang, Yue
Wang, Wei
Sun, Ying
Ma, Pinyi
Song, Daqian
Wang, Xinghua
coredata.dc:description: A simple and sensitive fluorescence assay was established for the effective monitoring of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity in serum based on the inner filter effect (IFE). The facile-synthesized silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs), as the fluorescent indicator of this sensing system, were prepared via a one-pot strategy using N-[3-(trimethoxysiyl)propyl]-ethylenediamine (DAMO) and catechol as the silicon source and reductant, respectively. The zymolyte of this assay, 4-nitrophenyl phosphate (PNPP), would be hydrolyzed with the catalyzation of ALP, resulting in the production of p-nitrophenol (PNP). PNP would induce a significant IFE on the fluorescence emitting from SiNPs due to the overlap of absorption spectrum of PNP and fluorescence excitation spectrum of SiNPs, which was employed to evaluate the activity of ALP. The limit of detection (LOD) and linear range for the present assay are 0.0027 U/L and 0.01–5.0 U/L, respectively. Inorganic ions, enzymes and other biological compounds commonly coexisting in human serum exhibited little influence on the present assay. Good correlation (r = 0.97) between the testing results of real serum samples obtained by the present assay and a commercial reagent kit demonstrates the reliability of this assay. By introducing the enzyme-catalyzed hydrolytic reaction for the generation of energy absorber of fluorescence excitation light, the specific and sensitive activity detection of target enzyme was realized in this work. Moreover, excellent analytical performance of the present assay exhibits broad potential of the facile-prepared fluorescent SiNPs for bioanalysis and clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP)
Fluorescence
Inner filter effect (IFE)
Silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs)
Human serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ALP activity in human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image and size distribution of SiNPs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of SiNPs at various excitation wavelengths. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of SiNPs, PNP, PNPP and mixture of SiNPs with PNP, and fluorescence excitation spectrum of SiNPs.
Fluorescence resistances of SiNPs to (a) long-time storage, (b) light radiation at 420 nm, and chemical environment with (c) high salt (NaCl) and (d) various pH levels.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of SiNPs. High resolution XPS spectra of (b) full range and (c) Si 2p, (d) O 1s, (e) C 1s, and (f) N 1s.

(a) Effects of several coexisting substances on fluorescence emissions of SiNPs. Concentrations of ALP, PNPP, DEA, Mg2+, and PNP are 100 U/L, 10 mM, 1 M, 1 mM, and 10 mM, respectively. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of PNPP in absence and presence of ALP at various concentration levels. Concentration of PNPP is 166 μM. (c) Fluorescence lifetime of SiNPs in presence and absence of PNP.
Effects of (a) Mg2+ concentration, (b) reaction time, (c) PNPP concentration and (d) pH level on IFE.

Influences of various coexisting substances on IFE. Concentrations of GLU, ATP, BSA, Tyr and Cys are 250 mg/L. Activity levels of GOX, HRP and TG are 250 U/L. Activity levels of ALP and ACP are 10 U/L and 100 U/L, respectively. Concentration of each metal ion is 0.1 mM. Excitation wavelength is 420 nm.
(a) Comparison and (b) correlation relationship of detection results of ALP activities in real human serum samples obtained by the proposed assay and a commercial reagent kit.

Fluorescence spectra of SiNPs upon addition of ALP with various activities (from top to bottom: 0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 8.0, 10, 12, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, and 100 U/L). (b) Plots and linear relationship (shown in inset) of Rfd versus ALP activity. Concentrations of PNPP, DEA and MgCl2 are 5 mM, 1 M and 0.4 mM, respectively. Excitation wavelength is 420 nm. Incubation temperature and time are 37 °C and 60 min, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of SiNPs upon addition of inhibitor with various concentrations (from bottom to top: 0, 1.95, 3.91, 7.81, 15.63, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, and 1000 μM). (b) Plots and linear relationship (shown in inset) of inhibition ratio versus inhibitor concentration. Inhibitor ratio I was calculated according to following equation: I (%) =
F - F
0
F B
- F
0
* 100 % , where F, F0 , and FB refer to the fluorescence intensities of the testing system containing both ALP and inhibitor, only ALP, and none of ALP and inhibitor, respectively. Excitation wavelength is 420 nm.
Sensing mechanism of the present assay.
Comparison of analytical performance of the proposed assay with other methods for ALP activity detection.

row.entry: Surface modification of the fluorescence indicator

GQDs-based Colorimetric and fluorescent
dual-mode sensing probe [51]
Dye-modified Eu3+-doped oxide nanoparticles-based fluorescence probe [54]
Heptamethine cyanine dye modified Eu3+-doped oxide nanoparticles

head.ce:title: A simple and sensitive assay of alkaline phosphatase activity in serum by fluorescent silicon nanoparticles based on inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun 130012, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun 130012, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and sensitive fluorescence assay was established for the effective monitoring of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity in serum based on the inner filter effect (IFE). The facile-synthesized silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs), as the fluorescent indicator of this sensing system, were prepared via a one-pot strategy using N-[3-(trimethoxysiyl)propyl]-ethylenediamine (DAMO) and catechol as the silicon source and reductant, respectively. The zymolyte of this assay, 4-nitrophenyl phosphate (PNPP), would be hydrolyzed with the catalyzation of ALP, resulting in the production of p-nitrophenol (PNP). PNP would induce a significant IFE on the fluorescence emitting from SiNPs due to the overlap of absorption spectrum of PNP and fluorescence excitation spectrum of SiNPs, which was employed to evaluate the activity of ALP. The limit of detection (LOD) and linear range for the present assay are 0.0027 U/L and 0.01–5.0 U/L, respectively. Inorganic ions, enzymes and other biological compounds commonly coexisting in human serum exhibited little influence on the present assay. Good correlation (r = 0.97) between the testing results of real serum samples obtained by the present assay and a commercial reagent kit demonstrates the reliability of this assay. By introducing the enzyme-catalyzed hydrolytic reaction for the generation of energy absorber of fluorescence excitation light, the specific and sensitive activity detection of target enzyme was realized in this work. Moreover, excellent analytical performance of the present assay exhibits broad potential of the facile-prepared fluorescent SiNPs for bioanalysis and clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is a kind of hydrolase found across a multitude of organisms, prokaryotes and eukaryotes alike [1]. The function of ALP is to catalyze the hydrolysis of biological compounds for the dephosphorylation of phosphoryl esters from them [2,3]. In the blood of human, the level of ALP depends on many factors, such as age, gender, blood type and pregnancy status, usually varying from 40 to 190 U/L [4]. ALP can provide optimal catalytic capacity in an alkaline media (typically pH = 9.8) [5–8]. By serving as an important biomarker for medical diagnosis, the abnormal level of ALP in serum indicates osteoporosis, periodontitis, leukemia, breast or prostatic cancer, and other diseases of human body [9–13]. Moreover, ALP is also frequently used as signal enzyme in clinical assays [14]. Therefore, it is necessary and meaningful to establish effective and convenient methods for monitoring ALP activity in human serum.

Up to now, a series of analytical methods, such as chromatography [15], colorimetry [13,16], electrochemistry [1], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [13,17], localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [18], and fluorometry [4,19], have been developed to detect ALP activity in various types of samples. However, compared with other methods which were commonly limited in practical applications of clinical diagnosis due to their disadvantages of complicated pre-treatment procedures or expensive instruments [3,20–23], fluorometry enjoys exceptional advantage owing to its excellent sensitivity, selectivity, facility and instantaneity [19]. Fluorescent materials for sensing ALP are mainly divided into two major catalogues: dyes and nano-materials. Compared with the former mostly synthesized intricately, the latter is more attractive for its easy preparation and superior quantum yield of fluorescence, chemical stability and aqueous solubility [24]. Among the routine mechanisms employed in development of fluorescence sensing methods, inner filter effect (IFE) can eliminate environmental interference and background noises efficiently [25–27], due to the facile formation or removal of spectral overlaps between the absorption of a substance participated in the sensing progress and the excitation or emission of a fluorophore. Currently, fluorescent nano-materials involving in sensing ALP based on IFE include gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) [20], carbon quantum dots (CDs) [26], CdTe/CdS QDs [28], and Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots [29]. However, only a few fluorescent nano-materials are suitable for biological analysis, bioimaging or vivo sensing, since most of reported fluorescent nano-materials exhibit biological incompatibility, poor water-solubility, and even toxicity [4]. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are the most commonly used nano-material in biological fields, which have been characterized as fluorescent materials with excellent biocompatibility and low biotoxicity, but inferior photoluminescence quantum yield (mostly lower 10 %) or doped toxic heteroatoms limit their applications [26]. Therefore, it is more favorable for bioanalysis to evolve new types of fluorescent nano-materials with good availability and biocompatibility, high photoluminescence efficiency and low toxicity.

Recently, silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs), as new type fluorescent nanomaterials, have attracted more and more attentions owing to their intrinsic properties (e.g., strong fluorescence and robust photostability) and distinct dominances (e.g., low cost, excellent biocompatibility and good biodegradability) [30–32]. Coupled with facile surface modifications and effective bioimaging techniques, SiNPs are regarded as promising alternatives to the routine organic dyes and toxic heavy-metal-containing quantum dots for in vitro and in vivo fluorescence analyses [31,33,34]. Remarkably, more and more fluorescent SiNPs with superhigh photoluminescence quantum yield even up to ∼100 % have been synthesized [33,35–37], and, it had been reported that some types of SiNPs can be biodegradable in vivo (∼8 h) [38–40]. Consequently, water-soluble SiNPs have been synthesized and employed for bioimaging [36,38,41], but their applications in detections of biological analytes are still scarce.

In this work, a facile and sensitive fluorescent sensing system was newly developed for detecting ALP activity in serum (Scheme 1 ), based on the IFE of p-nitrophenol (PNP) on the fluorescence emitted from water-soluble SiNPs synthesized by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. During the sensing process, PNP was generated by the ALP-catalyzed dephosphorylation of 4-nitrophenyl phosphate (PNPP). Because of the overlap between the absorption spectrum of PNP and the fluorescence excitation spectrum of SiNPs, fluorescence emitted from SiNPs would be suppressed gradually with the production of PNP, which was positively related to the activity of ALP. The proposed assay was used to determine ALP activities of real human serum samples and, the results were examined by using a commercial reagent kit. The comparison of analytical performance obtained by the proposed assay and the similarly reported methods was also performed. All experimental results demonstrated the excellent specificity, sensitivity, practicality and reliability of this assay.

Alkaline phosphate from calf intestinal mucosa (10,000 U/ml) was provided by New England Biolabs (Beingjing), Ltd. N-[3-(trimethoxysiyl)propyl]-ethylenediamine (DAMO), 4-nitrophenyl phosphate disodium salt hexahydrate (PNPP), diethanolamine (DEA) and sodium orthovanadate (Na3VO4) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. P-nitrophenol (PNP) was obtained from Shanghai Lingjin Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O) and catechol were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Glucose (GLU), adenosine triphosphate (ATP), tyrosine (Tyr), l-cysteine (Cys), bovine serum albumin (BSA), transglutaminase (TG), glucose Oxidase (GOX), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), and acid phosphatase (ACP) were from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Commercial ALP reagent kit (A059-1) was supplied by Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ cm was prepared by a Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA). Standard solutions of ALP at various activity levels were obtained freshly by dilution with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) before use.

Six serum samples from six healthy adult volunteers were provided by the Third Hospital of Jilin University (Changchun, China) and were diluted fifty-folds with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) solution before analyses.

The SiNPs for the indication of fluorescence in the proposed assay were prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method according to a previous report [42]. Briefly, 2.0 mL of DAMO and 11.0 mg of catechol were added to 10.0 mL of deionized water sequentially under stirring. After 3 min, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined reactor and heat-processed in an oven at 200 °C for 4 h. Subsequently, the product of the hydrothermal reaction was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da against ultrapure water for 7 h. Finally, the purified SiNPs were stored at 4 °C for further use. The quantum yield of the SiNPs emitting a green fluorescence was calculated to be about 11.1 %. The as-prepared SiNPs were diluted 100-folds with DEA buffer (pH = 9.8) solution freshly before use.

Detection of ALP activity in a standard solution or a real serum sample was carried out as follows. Firstly, 100 μL of DEA buffer (pH = 9.8) solution, 100 μL of MgCl2 (0.4 mM) solution, 200 μL of PNPP (5 mM) solution, and 100 μL of ALP standard solution or real serum sample were sequentially added into a 2.0 mL microcentrifugal tube. After being shaken gently, the tube was incubated at 37 °C for 60 min. Then, 50 μL of diluted SiNPs was injected into the above reaction solution and the mixture was shaken thoroughly. At last, the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture were measured in a 1-cm quartz cuvette under room temperature at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm.

To verify the dynamic response of the present assay towards the activity of ALP, an acknowledged inhibitor of ALP, Na3VO4, was employed to obtain gradient activity changes of ALP in sample solutions. Briefly, 200 μL of DEA buffer (pH = 9.8) solution, 200 μL of MgCl2 (0.4 mM) solution, 200 μL of PNPP (5 mM) solution, 100 μL of ALP (10 U/L) solution, and 300 μL of Na3VO4 solution at various concentration levels from 0 to 1000 μM were added into a microcentifugal tube, followed by gentle shaking and incubation at 37 °C for 60 min. The following detection operations were similar to the above for detection of ALP activity in a standard solution.

For most experiments related to the determination of ALP activity in this work, ratio of fluorescence decrease (Rfd) calculated according to the following equation was employed to indicate the efficiency of IFE.
R f d =
(
F 0
- F )
F 0
* 100 %
In this equation, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities obtained by testing blank samples and standard (or real) samples, respectively.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded by an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on a Cary 60 UV–vis spectrometer (Agilent Technologies Inc., USA). Morphology of the SiNPs was characterized by a Hitachi 8100 transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV without staining. Spectra of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) absorption were collected by an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA) equipped with a mono X-Ray source of Al Kα (1486.6 eV) and a Nicolet Avatar360 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA) in the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1, respectively. All pH values of buffer solutions were verified by using a PHS-3C pH-Meter (INESA Scientific Inc., China).
The morphology of the as-prepared SiNPs (Fig. 1 ) exhibits good dispersibility and size uniformity with an average diameter of 3.3 nm (inset of Fig. 1).

Under the excitation of incident light, the as-prepared SiNPs emitted bright green fluorescence with a maximum emission wavelength at 512 nm (Fig. 2 a), which was not shifted by varying the excitation wavelength in the range of 350−460 nm, indicating there is no size-dependent emission behavior of the SiNPs. The maximum fluorescence excitation wavelength of the SiNPs was at 420 nm (Fig. 2a, 2b). After storing for 3 months, the as-prepared SiNPs showed only slight decrease of the fluorescence emission performance (Fig. 3 a). The fluorescence resistance of the SiNPs to both long-time photobleaching and high salt were investigated (Fig. 3b and c). It can be seen that the SiNPs possess good stability and suitability for practical bioanalysis. Study of the effect of environmental pH on the fluorescence intensity emitted from SiNPs (Fig. 3d) revealed that alkaline pH would benefit the fluorescence emission, which may be due to the surface charge variation of SiNPs induced by the deprotonation process of the chemical groups on the surface of SiNPs [43], and, which exactly facilitates the analytical performance improvement of the proposed assay since the assays of ALP activities are commonly carried out under alkaline environment.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of SiNPs (Fig. 2b) displayed two characteristic absorption peaks appeared respectively at 290 and 425 nm. The former is referred to the π-π* transition of the CC bond, and the latter, which is usually associated with the passivated surface defects of nanoparticles, may be generated from the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface defects of SiNPs [44].

The functional groups attached on the surface of SiNPs were identified by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the as-prepared SiNPs demonstrated an obvious absorption band of the OH stretching vibration at 3404 cm−1 [33]. The characteristic absorption peaks corresponded to the stretching and bending vibrations of unsaturated C–H appeared at 2932 and 1471 cm−1, respectively [42]. The bands at 1608 cm−1 and 781 cm−1 were assigned to the bending and wagging vibrations of NH, respectively [45]. In addition, the presence of band at 1348 cm−1 was originated from the CN stretching vibration [42]. The sharp bands at 1122 cm−1 and 1028 cm−1 were referred to the Si-O-Si stretching vibration [34]. The band at 931 cm−1 was attributed to the Si-O stretching vibration of SiOH group, and the band at 691 cm-1 was related to a secondary amine [42]. The above results indicated that the as-prepared SiNPs were abounded with hydroxyl and amino groups. The presences of these functional groups were in favor of the water dispersibility and stability of the as-prepared SiNPs.

XPS measurement was performed for analyzing the elemental composition of the as-prepared SiNPs. Five major peaks appeared in the full range XPS spectra (Fig. 4b) at 102.2, 153.3, 284.8, 398.8, and 531.9 eV, which were in sequence assigned to Si 2p, Si 2s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s [36]. There were three types of silicon atoms which could be seen in the high resolution Si 2p spectra (Fig. 4c): SiC (101.0 eV), SiN (101.7 eV), and SiO (102.5 eV) [46]. The XPS spectrum of O 1s could be described by three peaks shown in Fig. 4d, the first two (531 eV and 532.5 eV) were correspondingly attributed to CO and CO groups, and the third (533.7 eV) was attributed to SiO group [31]. Five peaks shown in the high resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 4e) were severally associated with C-Si (283.9 eV), CC/C C (284.5 eV), CN (285.35 eV), COH/COC (286.1 eV) and CO (287.4 eV) groups [4,26,31]. In the XPS spectra of N 1s (Fig. 4f), three fitted peaks at 398.4, 399.0, and 401.1 eV were assigned to NSi, CNC, and N(C)3 groups, respectively [47–49]. These results were in consistent with those obtained by FT-IR.

The mechanism of the present sensing system can be interpreted by the simple IFE of PNP on the fluorescence emitted from SiNPs (Scheme 1). As shown in Fig. 2b, the maximum absorption wavelength of PNP (∼ 400 nm) was very close to the maximum excitation wavelength of SiNPs (420 nm), that is to say, the overlap of the absorption spectrum of PNP and the fluorescence excitation spectrum of SiNPs was obvious.

Effects of other substances coexisting in the present sensing system, including PNPP, DEA, Mg2+, ALP or their mixtures, on the fluorescence emissions of the SiNPs were also examined (Fig. 5 a). Compared with PNP, which was generated in the mixture of (ALP + PNPP + DEA + Mg2+) and would induce a great decrease of fluorescence intensity, these substances only induced very slight fluorescence intensity changes. These results demonstrated that the variations of fluorescence in the present sensing system can be considered mainly as a result of the variation of PNP amount.

As shown in Fig. 5b, with the increasing activity of ALP in the mixture of (ALP + PNPP + DEA + Mg2+), the peak absorbance of PNPP (∼315 nm) decreased gradually, while the absorption peak (∼ 400 nm) referred to PNP increased significantly due to the incremental amount of PNP generated by the ALP-catalyzed hydrolytic reaction. Certainly, intensity variations of incident light would affect the fluorescence intensity emitted from SiNPs, since the excitation energy for the process of fluorescence was provided by the incident light. Therefore, the activity of ALP, which was the vital factor for the production of PNP during this assay, could be indicated by the variation of fluorescence intensity emitted from SiNPs. This is a typical application of IFE mechanism for quantitative analysis. In the present sensing system, it is not necessary to worry about the influence of the fluorescence emitted by PNP or PNPP molecules during assay, because the fluorescence quantum yields of these chemicals are very poor (0.096 % and 0.109 %, respectively), owing to the significant quenching ability of the -NO2 groups substituted on the benzene rings of these molecules.

To further confirm the as-proposed IFE mechanism, the effect of PNP on the fluorescence lifetime of the SiNPs was investigated (Fig. 5c). Results showed that there was little difference between the fluorescence lifetimes of the SiNPs in the absence (τ = 6.16 ns) and presence (τ = 6.99 ns) of PNP. As known to all, the fluorescence lifetime of a material is usually changed by the energy transfer between the excited fluorophores and other chemical substances, but is predictably unaffected by IFE [20]. So it can be concluded that the sensing mechanism of the present assay is IFE, which were also in agreement with previous reports [26,42,45].

In order to obtain the optimal analytical performance, five key experimental parameters involved in the proposed assay, including the pH of DEA buffer solutions, the incubation time, and the concentrations of magnesium ions (Mg2+) and PNPP, were investigated.

Magnesium ion (Mg2+) plays a significant role in promoting the enzymatic reaction of phosphatase [16]. An appropriate concentration of Mg2+ would promote the IFE. However, concentration of Mg2+ more than 0.4 mM demonstrated little influence on IFE (Fig. 6 a). Therefore, 0.4 mM of Mg2+ was selected in further assays. The incubation time was also optimized to make sure that the hydrolysis reaction would be performed thoroughly. During the optimal incubation time, the ALP in this assay should be exhausted completely, and no more PNP would be produced after that. Experimental results indicated that an incubation time of 60 min was preferred in this assay (Fig. 6b). The dynamic detection range of the assay was determined partly by the concentration of PNPP used as the zymolyte for the hydrolysis reaction. After investigation, a concentration of PNPP of 5 mM was chosen for the following assays according to the results showed in Fig. 6c. Alkaline condition is propitious to the catalytic reaction of ALP, so several pH values in alkaline range (7.5–11.0) were tested. Results indicated the pH value of 9.8 was optimal for the assay since the most significant IFE was observed under this pH environment (Fig. 6d). It is worthwhile to mention that in the proposed assay the DEA solution was chosen as the pH buffer solution, due to its good biocompatibility and particular capability to coordinate with Mg2+ for preventing the formation of Mg(OH)2 precipitate during the enzymatic hydrolysis [14]. Because the proposed assay was established for the determination of ALP activity in human serum samples, the normal temperature of human body, 37 °C, was set as the incubation temperature in future assays.

The interferences of several inorganic ions (K+, Na+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, and Mg2+), enzymes (ACP, HRP, GOX, and TG) and biomolecules (Tyr, Cys, BSA, Glucose and ATP) that may present in human serum were tested by this assay. As can be seen in Fig. 7 , there were no obvious fluorescence decrease induced by these coexisting substances, suggesting the good selectivity of this assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ALP activity in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicability and reliability of the proposed assay, it was applied to the detection of ALP activities in real human serum samples. Results were listed in Fig. 8 a. The correlation between these results and those obtained by a commercial reagent kit was calculated to test the reliability of the proposed assay (Fig. 8b). A correlation coefficient (r) of 0.97 suggested the good accuracy and reliability of the proposed assay for the analyses of real samples.

The fluorescence responses of the established assay towards ALP with various activities were depicted in Fig. 9 a. The fluorescent intensity emitted from the SiNPs decreased gradually as the ALP activity was continuously increased from 0 to 100 U/L. As shown in the inset of Fig. 9b, the calibration line of the assay was constructed by plotting the fluorescence intensity at 512 nm versus the ALP activity. The decrease of fluorescence intensity was well correlated with the increase of ALP activity in the range of 0.01–5.0 U/L. The detection limit (LOD) for this assay is 0.0027 U/L, which was calculated based on three times of the standard deviation (3σ) of the fluorescence response obtained by testing a blank sample, and the slope (S) of the calibration line.

As is well known, the enzyme inhibition assay is an important part in the applicability evaluation of a biological sensing system. As a common ALP inhibitor, Na3VO4 was employed for the following inhibiting assays. Fig. 10 a shows that the peak fluorescence intensity of the proposed assay increased gradually with the increasing amount of Na3VO4, owing to the restricted production of PNP from the hydrolysis reaction of PNPP resulted by the inhibition of ALP. The inhibition ability of Na3VO4 towards ALP was evaluated by a calibration line (Inset of Fig. 10b), which was constructed by plotting the inhibition ratios versus Na3VO4 concentrations. The concentration of Na3VO4 where the fluorescence response of this assay was reduced by half, IC50 value, was calculated to be 38.2 μM, which was well accorded with the reported values obtained by other methods (∼38.4 μM) [20,26]. These results indicated the good response effectiveness of this assay for the detection of ALP activity.

Furthermore, a comparison of analytical performance between the proposed assay and several recently reported strategies was performed (Table 1 ). Due to the advantages of its ultrahigh sensitivity, wide linear range, less operation for the construction of sensing system, and low-cost consumption of reagents, the assay proposed in this work is very suitable for the practical measurement of ALP activity in clinical diagnosis.

In this work, a novel and simple fluorescent assay for accurate and sensitive detection of ALP activity in human serum was established for the first time. Based on the IFE of PNP on the green-light fluorescence emission of facile-synthesized SiNPs, the established assay exhibits distinguished response towards ALP activity, since the amount of PNP produced by the hydrolysis reaction of PNPP was directly proportional to the activity of ALP. Compared with the preparation procedures of similar strategies based on spectroscopy, the fabrication of sensing system for this assay is cost-effective and easy, because there is no need for expensive reagents containing noble metals for syntheses of fluorescent nanoparticles and complicated surface modifications on nanoparticles. Good analytical performance of this assay, including the ultralow LOD of 0.0027 U/L, wide linear range of 0.01–5.0 U/L, and good correlation (r = 0.97) between the analytical results for real human serum samples obtained by it and a commercial reagent kit, indicate its reliability for practical application in clinical diagnosis. Furthermore, the established assay can also be applied to the determination of ALP inhibitors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors declare that there is no conflict of interest regarding the publication of this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the State Scholarship Fund of China (No. 201806175003), Science and Technology Developing Foundation of Jilin Province of China (Nos. 20180201050YY and 201701011106JC) and Industrial Innovation Funds of Jilin Province of China (No. 2018C034-1).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanostructured electrochemical immunosensor for detection of serological alkaline phosphatase
Colloids Surf. B Biointerfaces

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stimulus response of Au-NPs@GMP-Tb core-shell nanoparticles: toward colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensing of alkaline phosphatase activity in algal blooms of a freshwater lake
Environ. Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhang, J. Deng, Y. Xue, G. Shi, T. Zhou, Stimulus response of Au-NPs@GMP-Tb core-shell nanoparticles: toward colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensing of alkaline phosphatase activity in algal blooms of a freshwater lake, Environ. Sci. Technol., 50 (2016) 847-855.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent biosensor for phosphate determination based on immobilized polyfluorene-liposomal nanoparticles coupled with alkaline phosphatase
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Kahveci, M.J. Martinez-Tome, R. Mallavia, C.R. Mateo, Fluorescent biosensor for phosphate determination based on immobilized polyfluorene-liposomal nanoparticles coupled with alkaline phosphatase, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 9 (2017) 136-144.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots-based recyclable real-time fluorescence assay for alkaline phosphatase with adenosine triphosphate as substrate
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Qian, L. Chai, C. Tang, Y. Huang, J. Chen, H. Feng, Carbon quantum dots-based recyclable real-time fluorescence assay for alkaline phosphatase with adenosine triphosphate as substrate, Anal. Chem., 87 (2015) 2966-2973.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dan Li is currently a PhD student in College of Chemistry, Jilin University. Her interest is spectral analysis.
Yanxiao Jiang is currently a PhD student in College of Chemistry, Jilin University. his interest is chromatographic analysis.
Sihan Chen is currently a PhD student in College of Chemistry, Jilin University. Her interest is spectral analysis.
Qingnan Zhao is currently a PhD student in College of Chemistry, Jilin University. Her interest is spectral analysis.
Yue Zhang is currently a PhD student in College of Chemistry, Jilin University. Her interest is spectral analysis.
Wei Wang is currently a graduate student in College of Chemistry, Jilin University. Her interest is spectral analysis.
Ying Sun gained her doctor’s degree from College of Chemistry, Jilin University in 2009 and she is an associate professor in that school. Her research area is spectral analysis.
Pinyi Ma gained his doctor’s degree from College of Chemistry, Jilin University in 2017 and he is a engineer in that school. His research area is spectral analysis.
Daqian Song gained his doctor’s degree from College of Chemistry, Jilin University in 2003 and he is a professor in that school. His research areas are spectral and chromatography analysis.
Xinghua Wang gained his doctor’s degree from College of Chemistry, Jilin University in 2006 and he is a professor in that school. His research areas are spectral and chromatography analysis.
